data.title: Johnson & Johnson: Fairly Valued, But Recession-Resilient
data.description:
At the beginning of January 2025, I wrote my last article about Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) and in the article I rated the stock as a “Buy”. I argued that Johnson & Johnson’s stock could serve as a high-yield bond alternative and in the conclusion, I wrote:

In about two weeks, on January 22, 2025, the company will report fourth quarter results for fiscal 2024. Not only are analysts rather bullish right now with no “Sell” and “Strong Sell” ratings in the last 90 days and only one “Hold” rating, but we could argue that the downside is limited, and the stock price might not drop much lower than $135. On the other hand, we should also not expect high-growth rates or the stock price going to the moon. Johnson & Johnson could be a solid investment, paying a decent dividend yield and rather serve as a high-yield bond alternative.

The call was right as the stock increased about 20% in value in the following weeks. But similar to almost every other stock in the world, Johnson & Johnson declined in value in the last few days. Nevertheless, the stock still increased about 4% (and 5% when including dividends) while the S&P 500 declined 8.5% in the same timeframe.

Before Johnson & Johnson will report first quarter results for fiscal 2025 on Tuesday, April 15, 2025, let’s look at the company and stock once again. Especially considering how volatile the stock market was in the last few days and what implications tariffs and a potential recession might have, an update about Johnson & Johnson seems necessary.

Quarterly Results and Full-Year Results

Before we look at the potential future, let’s look back at the last results Johnson & Johnson reported on January 22, 2025, for the fourth quarter and full-year fiscal 2024. In the fourth quarter, sales as well as earnings per share did slightly beat estimates and when looking at the results year-over-year, sales increased from $21,395 million in Q4 '23 to $22,520 million in Q4 '24 – resulting in 5.3% year-over-year growth. But while sales increased, earnings before provision for taxes on income declined by 19.5% year-over-year from $4,826 million in the same quarter last year to $3,887 million in this quarter. Earnings per diluted share declined from $1.70 in Q4 '23 to $1.41 in Q4 '24 – resulting in a 17.1% year-over-year decline.

While we see Johnson & Johnson struggling a little bit in the fourth quarter (especially with operating income and earnings per share declining), full-year results are better. Sales increased from $85,159 million in fiscal 2023 to $88,821 million in fiscal 2024 – resulting in 4.1% year-over-year growth. Earnings before provision for taxes on income increased 10.8% year-over-year from $15,062 million in fiscal 2023 to $16,687 million in fiscal 2024. And diluted earnings per share from continuing operations increased from $5.20 in fiscal 2023 to $5.79 in fiscal 2024 – resulting in 11.3% year-over-year growth.

Johnson & Johnson is reporting in two major segments – Innovative Medicine, which generated $56,964 million in revenue in fiscal 2024 (64% of total revenue) and MedTech, which generated $31,857 million in fiscal 2024 (36% of total revenue). And both segments reported growth rates in the mid-single digits in fiscal 2024. When looking at the sub-segments, especially Oncology and Cardiovascular are worth mentioning and reporting high-growth rates.

Oncology generated $20,781 million in revenue in fiscal 2024 and the sub-segment grew revenue 17.7% year-over-year. Cardiovascular generated $7,707 million in fiscal 2024 and the sub-segment grew its revenue 21.4% year-over-year.

Growth

Johnson & Johnson is certainly not a high-growth business, and investors should not expect high-growth rates from Johnson & Johnson. And the problem becomes obvious when looking at the different segments. While Johnson & Johnson is growing at a very high pace in its two sub-segments oncology and cardiovascular, but since other sub-segments are not growing, the top line isn’t increasingly rapidly.

And management is also optimistic for fiscal 2025, but adjusted operational sales will grow only between 2.0% and 3.0%. Operational sales are expected to be in a range of $90.9 billion and $91.7 billion. Adjusted earnings per share are expected to be in a range of $10.50 to $10.70, resulting in 5.2% to 7.2% year-over-year growth.

For the first quarter of fiscal 2025, analysts are also not expecting Johnson & Johnson to grow at a high pace. Revenue is expected to be $21.59 billion, which would result in about 1% top-line growth compared to the same quarter last year. Earnings per share (on a GAAP basis) are expected to be $2.27 and, compared to $1.34 in the same quarter last year, it would actually result in a high-growth rate. But in my opinion, we should rather look at the adjusted earnings per share of $2.29 in the same quarter last year and in this case, the bottom-line would slightly decline.

Analysts have similar expectations for the years to come and assume earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 4.1% between fiscal 2024 and fiscal 2033. And I don’t think we have much reason to be more optimistic. I already mentioned above that Johnson & Johnson can grow in the double-digits in some parts of its business – for example oncology and cardiovascular – but due to the large and very diversified business it is not enough when one or two sub-segments are growing with a high pace. It is also not enough for a business generating $90 billion in revenue to introduce two or three new blockbuster drugs. And Johnson & Johnson certainly has a promising pipeline.

Aside from growing by improving the top line, Johnson & Johnson – like every other business – can also grow by lowering expenses and improving efficiency. But I don’t think we should expect margin improvement to contribute in a significant way to bottom-line growth. I think it is possible for slightly higher margins to contribute about 1% growth annually, but we should not be too optimistic. Johnson & Johnson was trading for stable margins in the last few decades (which is actually a good sign for stability).

Additionally, Johnson & Johnson can use share buybacks to grow the bottom line and, in the past, the company has used share buybacks – but it was never a big part of its capital allocation strategy. During the last 20 years, the number of shares outstanding was reduced with a CAGR of 1.14%, and we can assume that share buybacks will contribute about 1% to bottom-line growth.

Overall, it is difficult to imagine that Johnson & Johnson will grow at a higher rate than in the low-to-mid single digits. In the last ten years, Johnson & Johnson grew its revenue with a CAGR of 1.80%. Operating income, however, increased only with a CAGR of 0.47% and earnings per share grew only 0.16% annually in the last ten years.

Recession

Looking at the growth rates and upside potential is certainly important, but we should also not ignore the flip side – the downside risk a business is facing. And not only due to the tariffs announced in the last few days we should always consider what impact a potential recession might have on a business.

When looking at the stock price performance of Johnson & Johnson in the last few decades, we see several drawdowns, but the steepest decline was in the 1970s when the stock fell 52.5% from its previous all-time high. All following corrections and bear markets took down Johnson & Johnson only about 40% or less and compared to many other stocks this is rather moderate.

Especially when looking at the S&P 500 in comparison, we see Johnson & Johnson performing much better in most cases. And performing better can have many different meanings: Usually it means that an investment in a certain stock is leading to a higher return and Johnson & Johnson slightly outperformed the S&P 500 over the last 30 years when including dividends.

But right now, the decisive question is not whether a stock can outperform the index during the bull run, the question right now is how we can limit the downside risk during bear markets and a potential recession. And Johnson & Johnson as a healthcare business might be able to perform well again during a downturn.

When looking at revenue as well as earnings per share in the last decades, we can’t claim that Johnson & Johnson was not affected at all by a recession, but it is performing solid. When excluding the years 2020 and the following, revenue hardly declined and earnings per share declined steeply on several occasions – but in almost no case we can make a connection between the declining earnings per share and a recession.

The argument for healthcare and pharmaceutical companies is very simple: The products Johnson & Johnson is selling are not only essential, but in some cases even essential for survival. Of course, surgeries can sometimes be postponed or in some cases it is possible to switch to cheaper alternatives for some pharmaceuticals. Nevertheless, a major part of Johnson & Johnson’s revenue can be seen as almost guaranteed.

I think we will see the fiscal guidance of countless companies being revised and analysts will also lower targets. In the case of Johnson & Johnson, we can be optimistic that the company will be able to meet its fiscal 2025 guidance.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

An important step in every analysis is to determine an intrinsic value for a stock to reach a decision if a stock is over- or undervalued. And for a first quick overview, I usually look at the price-free-cash-flow ratio and the price-earnings ratio. In the case of Johnson & Johnson, both valuation multiples indicate that Johnson & Johnson is not really expensive, but it is also not cheap or a bargain.

At the time of writing, Johnson & Johnson is trading for 25.6 times earnings, which seems rather expensive for a business growing only in the low-to-mid single digits, and it is also above the 5-year average of 23.10. When looking at the P/FCF ratio, Johnson & Johnson is trading for a valuation multiple of 19.95, which seems a little more acceptable and is also below the 5-year average of 22.63.

Especially with the recent crash, we can find several other stocks trading for similar valuation multiples – but these stocks are often growing at a much higher pace.

As always, we are not only looking at these simple valuation multiples but also use a discount cash flow calculation to determine an intrinsic value for the stock. Once again, we are calculating with a 10% discount rate as this is the minimum annual return we like to achieve. Additionally, we calculate with 2,429 million diluted shares outstanding and the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was $18.06 billion.

Calculating with these assumptions, Johnson & Johnson has to grow its free cash flow slightly below 5% annually from now till perpetuity to be fairly valued. And considering the growth assumptions we made above, we can argue that these growth rates are reasonable, but I would also not assume that Johnson & Johnson is able to grow at a much higher pace.

However, I would argue that the free cash flow of the last four quarters might be a little bit too pessimistic as a basis. In the last four quarters, JNJ reported a free cash flow margin of 20.3% (meaning 20.3% of revenue ended up as free cash flow). The average of the last ten years, however, was 22.4%, and we can assume some margin improvement here. When assuming that 22.4% of fiscal 2025 revenue (according to guidance) will end up as free cash flow and assuming 5% growth from now till perpetuity, we get an intrinsic value of $168.39, and we can see Johnson & Johnson as slightly undervalued at this point. However, it is also possible that 5% annual growth till perpetuity is a little too optimistic for Johnson & Johnson over the long run.

Conclusion

I would argue that Johnson & Johnson can be seen as fairly valued, and it is certainly not the perfect investment. But in a time of very high levels of uncertainty, limited stability and high volatility on the stock market, Johnson & Johnson might be a business worth considering.

I still am not expecting high-growth rates from Johnson & Johnson in the years to come, and the stock is certainly not a stock likely to double or triple in a few years. But I consider the stock as an investment that will provide some level of stability in volatile times and an investment with limited downside risk. Johnson & Johnson, as one of the major healthcare and pharmaceutical businesses might be more recession-resilient than many other businesses and over the next few years, this might actually be one of the most important criteria for any investment.

data.title: IGD: Capital Erosion And Shrinking Dividend Payouts
data.description:
Overview

Voya Global Equity Dividend and Premium Opportunity Fund (NYSE:IGD) operates as a diversified option ETF that aims to provide attractive total returns and provide shareholders with a high level of income. I've grown to enjoy the stability and income that option ETFs can provide. However, I've realized that not all option ETFs are constructed the same. IGD has an inception dating back to 2005, which means we have over two decades worth of performance data to reference. Additionally, the ETF has a reasonable expense ratio of 0.97%.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that IGD's price has fallen by about 0.9%, with most of the decline happening over the last few weeks. When including all the distributions that have been paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 10.4% over the same period. One of the main appeals of a fund like IGD is the generous starting dividend yield of 11.7%. Distributions are paid out on a monthly basis, which can make it an attractive option for retired investors seeking a supplemental form of dividend income. However, the high income comes with inherent flaws that make me cautious about the long-term performance of the fund.

Although the income from IGD may be attractive, the fund has a poor history of consistently eroding investor capital. Additionally, the most recent reporting documents indicate that the underlying earnings aren't enough to actually support the distributions. Most of the distributions come from return of capital, which can expedite NAV decay when markets are declining. So let's start by taking a look at IGD's underlying fund strategy that it implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the fund's overview, IGD has net assets of $488M that are spread across 237 different holdings. The fund takes a global approach and focuses on companies that have a history of attractive dividend yields. The global exposure can be a great way to slowly diversify your portfolio away from the US indexes. 69% of the fund's assets are still US-based, but there are varying amounts of capital invested across Asia and Europe.

We can see that investments in Financials comprise the largest portion of the fund at 25.77%. This is followed by investments in Industrials and Health Care companies, accounting for 15.06% and 13.71% of assets respectively. However, the combination of these three sectors comprise over 54% of total assets, so we can assume that these will have the greatest impact on IGD's daily price changes and total return potential.

The fund doesn't explicitly state that it puts a focus on dividend growth, but some underlying holdings indicate that it's an important part of the strategy. For instance, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) is the largest position within IGD, with a weight of 1.66% of assets. For reference, JNJ has been able to raise its dividend for 62 consecutive years without disruption. This is followed by positions in The Procter & Gamble Company (PG) and AbbVie Inc. (ABBV), accounting for 1.65% and 1.60% of assets respectively. PG is another dividend king who has raised dividends for 68 consecutive years without disruption. Just for reference, here are some notable dividend traits of the top holdings:

  • RTX Corporation (RTX): 31 consecutive years of dividend raises with a payout ratio of only 43%.
  • PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP): 52 consecutive years of dividend raises with a 5-year dividend CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 7.25%.

However, I can't help but think that the focus on dividend growth is a bit obsolete here since IGD chooses to implement an option overlay strategy. This means that management writes options against portions of their underlying holdings in an effort to generate additional income for shareholders. These options produce premiums, which are used to fund the distributions. The fund reporting seems to indicate that IGD deploys an OTM (out-of-the-money) option strategy, which allows for a greater amount of upside potential. However, the growth is ultimately capped to the selected strike price of the option. Therefore, I expect IGD to underperform traditional ETFs that track indices like the S&P 500 (SPX).

Valuation & Risk Profile

The inclusion of an option strategy is one of the contributing factors to IGD's price decline. Looking at the performance of IGD since inception, the share price has declined by over 74.4%. The fund has decimated investor's capital and is a clear indication that the underlying strategy may be ineffective. Even though the total return when including distributions is now above 122%, I do not believe this is worth the risk of seeing your invested capital erode over time. The history of eroding capital is what ultimately stops me from suggesting this fund over a long time horizon.

In a hypothetical scenario, if an investor committed $10,000 into IGD, their position would now be valued at approximately $2,500. However, the investor would have received a little over $12,000 back in distributions over the two decades. Two decades is a long time to wait to get your initial capital back.

IGD operates as a closed-end fund so that prices can trade at a difference from the actual value of underlying assets. As a result, the price currently trades at a discount to NAV of 8.6%. Looking back over the last decade below, IGD has exclusively traded in the discount territory and has never crossed into the premium to NAV territory, even through bull markets. This could be an indication that the market doesn't deem IGD as a valuable fund. In an effort to determine whether or not this discount to NAV pricing is an opportunity, I took a look at different reporting documents.

The most up-to-date report is the annual report dated from August 2024. The fund generates its earnings through a combination of net investment income and net realized gains from its underlying holdings. The report indicates that the biggest risk would be that IGD has failed to generate a sufficient amount of earnings to fully support distributions. This has ultimately led to the fund's NAV decreasing over the last decade. For instance, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2015: $9.31
  • 2016: $7.52
  • 2017: $8.01
  • 2018: $8.03
  • 2019: $7.02
  • 2020: $6.26
  • 2021: $6.01
  • 2022: $6.36
  • 2023: $5.97
  • 2024: $6.12

The declining NAV is the clearest indication that the strategy is ultimately ineffective over a long stretch of time. While there were some slight upticks on the NAV for some years, they weren't enough to ultimately change the momentum to a positive sentiment.

However, I will give IGD credit where it's due. The fund has proven to be more resilient during downturns because of the option strategy. For instance, markets declined throughout 2022 because of a Fed rate shock. The Fed aggressively increased interest rates to combat record-high inflationary data. Markets declined and traditional index-tracking ETFs such as SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY) and Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ). For instance, SPY saw a total loss of 18.17% and QQQ experienced a loss of 32.58%. In comparison, IGD was a lot more resilient and had a total loss of only 5%.

Dividend Decreases

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.05 per share, the current dividend yield sits at around 11.7%. If we multiply the current monthly payout by twelve, we get an estimated annual payout of $0.60 per share. However, the dividend history over the last decade clearly indicates that payouts have continued to decrease over time. This leads me to believe that the payouts are ultimately too generous, and the fund could benefit from a reduction.

While management has reduced payouts over time, I still don't think the reductions have been strong enough. The fund is in a bit of a difficult spot because if they severely reduce distributions, it sort of defeats the purpose of the fund. If they don't reduce the payouts, the NAV may continue to deteriorate over the next decade if changes aren't made.

This is reinforced by the latest section 19(a) notice that indicates 90% of the distributions have been funded by return of capital, while only 10% of the distributions were actually funded by net investment income. Dependence on return of capital can indicate that IGD hasn't actually been able to generate earnings that support the distribution. The only benefit to return of capital is that the distributions aren't actually taxed. Instead, an investor's cost basis is reduced and taxes are deferred until the time of sale.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate IGD as a sell because of the continued capital erosion, NAV declines, and shrinking dividend payouts. While the fund does have exposure to high-quality companies, the distribution policy appears to be too generous. The large distributions have caused the fund's NAV to decay over time. While management continues to decrease payouts, it hasn't really helped the fund regain some positive momentum in its NAV. I do not believe the high dividend yield is worth the risks here.

data.title: CoreWeave: One Round Before It's Game Over (Upgrading To Buy)
data.description:
CoreWeave, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:CRWV) stock chart resembles a kindergarten-written "M," shown in the following chart of the stock since going public in late March against the S&P 500 (SP500) and Nebius Group (NBIS). The stock shot down on the day of its IPO, Friday, by around 7%, then surged around 65% between Monday and Wednesday from $37.80 to $61.36 per share. Since its 52-week-high of $64.62 per share, the stock has crashed over 31%, specifically getting dragged down on Thursday, with the stock closing over 13% lower for the day.

We're upgrading CoreWeave to a buy for the short term; we called sell on the stock after its IPO, and our bearish call played out pretty well, as shown below, with the stock down 12% versus the S&P 500 up roughly 4%.

Our bearish thesis of renting GPUs as a service business model remains valid for the mid-to-long run, but we think the stock could outperform in the near-term because of the logic of IPOs.

CoreWeave management, while yes, finance guys rather than tech guys, wouldn't take the company public if they don't have the pipeline to support growth. So, our thesis is about a structural flaw for this neo-cloud peer group in the longer term is still in play. However, CoreWeave, in particular, should be okay for the near term, considering this current continued optimism and the fact that the current negatives have been recognized by the market. For now, where AI is concerned, all the tier 1 guys are holding on to their 2025 cloud capex. The structural problem with CoreWeave and its peers, i.e., Nebius Group, Crusoe, Lambda Labs, or Together AI, is that these companies have to keep spending, or more formally, they need to have upfront capex, or they are dead. If they don't spend overtime, revenue will drop. We think that, in the short term, the pipeline to support growth is there, and the stock should trade higher.

The reason we specify consistent spend to see revenue growth is that when it comes to renting out GPUs, they can only slap a higher average selling price or ASP when the GPU is fresh, i.e., they quickly lose ASP power over time on older tech. The only players that can afford to keep up that higher spend are the tier 1 guys, so while Microsoft is now a top customer, it will end up being CoreWeave's competitor and Amazon's (AMZN) AWS is already a competitor listed on SemiAnalysis' chart. Now, tariffs make things worse because tariffs going up means expenses will go up, and spending is already a point of concern for CoreWeave, with a debt burden of $8B. But, this also shouldn't weigh on the coming quarter, so we think for now this is an attractive swing trade.

It's all about the near-term pipeline

Now, our buy call is for a swing trade, not a long-term position. The selling point, or more accurately, buying point, for CoreWeave now is the idea that the company has a strong pipeline for the near-term. The rumors about Microsoft (MSFT) cutting or backing off of deals have yet to be confirmed, so they signal future troubles rather than near-term ones. And, anyway, the market has priced them in. The news about Google (GOOGL)(GOOG) being close to agreeing with CoreWeave on a deal, reported by The Information, could also be a booster if confirmed on this quarter's earnings call.

The company also announced a $4.5B deal and 15-year lease agreement with Galaxy Digital "to host its infrastructure at the latter's Helios campus in West Texas." Galaxy Digital has basically changed itself from a crypto mine data center to host CoreWeave as an AI provider. The deal basically means that Galaxy will give 133MW of critical IT Load to host AI and HPC infrastructure for Corewweave, and all capacity should be set up by the end of 1H26. There is also the deal closed with OpenAI in March on the table, which further highlights that this is the go-to guy with the neoclouds. So, we think there is trouble for the neocloud in the midterm, but we think CoreWeave is in a well trafficked area for the short term, so it should be okay.

Our thesis on Tech Contrarians remains to see a pullback from the tier 1 capex spend for the year, and that includes CoreWeave's largest customer, Microsoft, but the fact remains that Microsoft is holding on to that $80B figure for the year. Google just yesterday reiterated its $75B despite panic on AI demand compared to spend. On its S-1, the company reported remaining performance obligations or RPOs of $15.1B. CoreWeave also got higher visibility than its competition when it comes to clients, and that should show up in the coming earnings report.

Our advice is to buy the stock below $42 levels, which we think we'll see happen, and step out once the stock surges on its near-term pipeline, considering market optimism on the stock is waning, so a boost in confidence shouldn't be impossible for the current quarter's report. The insider trading activity seems to be doing a similar swing trade strategy, although, on a much shorter time span, the following outlines the trading activity news headlines on April 5th.

What we emphasize is that this is a name that is likely to see higher losses in the mid to long run due to its need and lack of upfront capex, high debt, and high dependence on older generation Nvidia (NVDA) AI GPUs. Once that near-term boost happens, that's when this turns into an attractive short opportunity. We'll downgrade CRWV stock once we see that pop because we think they'll win one round before it's game over.

data.title: Ethereum Needs A Defining Catalyst (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
So far this year, Ethereum (ETH-USD) has erased the gains it made in 2024 and has been on the hardest receiving end (among major altcoins) of the crypto sell-off we've witnessed in the past month. For comparison, on a YTD basis, ETH has lost ~55%, Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has lost ~16%, Solana (SOL-USD) is down by ~42%, while Ripple (XRP-USD) lost ~15%.

The spot Ethereum ETFs approved in May last year were a significant milestone for ETH; however, they've not had nearly as much demand as the spot Bitcoin ETFs. On the surface, this isn't surprising because Bitcoin has the strongest community and track record among crypto assets. When investors hold Bitcoin, they feel they own an asset that is widely regarded as a solid store of value. At least the U.S. government has said they won't be selling any more Bitcoin and plan to buy Bitcoin in the future. This cements investors’ confidence, at least for now, and might be a good incentive to hold BTC, especially in the current market turmoil.

Turning to the second most valued crypto, Ethereum, some key questions to revisit include: why should investors hold ETH or its spot ETFs? What incentives do investors have? With the market retrace we've seen so far this year, are there any catalysts left for Ethereum or developments in the pipeline that would incentivize investors to purchase and hold ETH or its ETPs? Let's get into these analyses in this article.

Staking Integration Into Spot Ethereum ETF Is A Potential Catalyst

At this point, ETH needs a strong catalyst to move the needle. The market generally lacks momentum right now, and I'm not overweighting ETH at this time, and think investors should monitor ETH sentiment closely. The YTD ETH-BTC chart doesn't look good. It is down ~47%

Amid this downturn, what could potentially move the needle for ETH by creating an incentive to buy and hold the asset and in turn create a lasting trend reversal not just dead cat bounces? I think staking integration into the spot Ethereum ETFs is one potential catalyst to watch out for.

The current SEC leadership has taken a more lenient approach toward crypto regulation, and I’d say it’s working to ease the bad blood between blockchain firms and regulators. There have been some surprising moves by the government and regulators lately, like the release of Ross Ulbricht, and the SEC dropping its investigations into certain crypto firms. The appeal to the XRP case was dropped by the SEC. This is just one instance of closed litigation. There have also been dismissals of some long-standing enforcement actions, like the enforcement action against Kraken and Consensys were dropped weeks ago. These concessions are great for the industry and send the signal that clarity is likely coming for every aspect of crypto that has had regulatory ambiguity.

The latest SEC statement that directly influences the industry as a whole and shows how crypto regulation is shaping up, is the declaration that certain Proof of Work (“PoW”) activities and cryptos earned from proof of work mechanisms or participation in mining pools do not constitute securities offerings under the federal securities laws. This clarity is a big relief for PoW cryptocurrencies and miners.

Now turning to the PoW cousin which is the Proof of Stake (“PoS”), which powers Ethereum and other major blockchains like Solana, there is need for a similar clarity surrounding PoS chains, and staking in particular. Such clarity will be one of the catalysts for a potential ETH rebound when market sentiment becomes much better and the downturn caused by the tariffs war blows over.

The topic of PoS staking triggering security laws has become an even hotter debate since the spot Ethereum ETF fund managers began filing amendments for staking approval in the ETFs. There have been several filings by the fund managers, pushing for staking approval. NYSE filed the latest amendment the previous week on behalf of Bitwise asking the SEC to allow staking in the Bitwise spot Ethereum ETF (ETHW). Just a slight digression here, I recently wrote about how Bitwise is in a better position to capture ETH ETF share if staking gets approved, because of their existing robust ETH staking infrastructure and past expertise in running an ETH staking ETP. You can read the ETHW article here.

The big question remains, will a similar clarity that has come for PoW chains likely come for PoS chains in the near future? Are there any inherent constraints to PoS networks that make such approval NOT remotely possible?

I’ll dive into some points in this piece, based on my analysis and perspective, and why I think there could be reconsiderations from regulators regarding PoS chains and PoS activities, and some arguments I think the ETH ETF fund managers will lean into, which will maybe lead to a potential staking approval in the ETFs, not also forgetting some valid counterpoints that would strengthen regulators' argument and stance. This article is meant to be an open conversation. Other supporting or opposing views are more than welcome; let’s make it a real discussion in the comments. The topic of ETF staking approval is still a bit of a gray area, even the current SEC leadership hasn’t given any clear signal yet, but that’s exactly what makes it worth digging into and what currently piques the curiosity of crypto investors.

Putting Ethereum Proof-of-Stake Through the Howey Test

There will probably be plenty of angles for ETH ETF filers to argue that staking shouldn’t trigger securities laws. The same goes for the SEC, which has precedent to argue that the pooling of assets and expectation of yield (staking rewards in this case), are enough to deny staking in the spot ETFs.

The Howey Test centers on four criteria - 1. an investment of money, 2. in a common enterprise, 3. with an expectation of profit, 4. primarily from the efforts of others. How will the fund managers circumvent the notion of expectation of profit, reliance on a common enterprise, and the centralization of validator operations (efforts of others)?

To get staking approved, I believe some staking structures will be off the table from the get-go. For example, Ethereum’s liquid staking, which has gained popularity since introduced after the Merge and has seen TVL grow strongly because of the advantages of liquidity and DeFi composability, will likely not be viable for consideration at all. The reason being liquid staking tokens represent a rebaseable or yield-bearing claim on staked ETH that is pooled and managed by third parties, which fits the SEC’s definition of an investment contract under Howey’s criteria of “reliance on a common enterprise.”

The fund managers would likely strategize to go all decentralized in their staking structure. This will greatly reduce counterparty risks, and such risk would be one of the core arguments for the SEC to deny approval of the staking. Bitwise is worth a second mention here, being the only issuer so far with an in-house staking structure that could probably be integrated seamlessly into the spot ETF structure. Fidelity (FETH) also self-custodies its ETH holdings for the spot Ethereum ETF, but Fidelity hasn't announced any in-house staking infrastructure yet.

To evade triggering the Howey criteria, the issuers might argue that investors are only putting money into ETH itself via the spot ETFs, and not directly into staking. The staking process is secondary to the investor’s core objective, which is exposure to ETH’s spot price. The fact that staking rewards are tacked on is more like a side benefit, just like dividends on stocks. The real asset the investor is backing is the underlying commodity, ETH, and staking isn’t their primary investment strategy. The rewards from staking would simply be passed through to investors in proportion to their shareholding. There is really no common enterprise beyond the primary function of managing the spot ETF. The funds aren’t running a business that actively profits from the staking pool, they just hold ETH and secure the Ethereum network technologically.

Staking is a technological necessity for the Ethereum network. And staking rewards aren't exactly speculative profit - it’s a reward for “work done,” which is to secure Ethereum’s network. Staking isn’t primarily about “making money” but about preserving the value of ETH by sustaining the network.

Risks and Conclusion

The preceding arguments are based on my interpretation and analysis of ETH within the Howey framework. While they highlight some passable arguments the issuers could present to secure approval of staking in the ETFs, the SEC also has precedent on its side and has long argued that staking yields from centralized operations trigger securities laws. However, with the current U.S. administration’s stance towards crypto and their seriousness about removing ambiguity surrounding crypto assets and their regulatory frameworks (at least from recent press releases, this has been their stance), I think an early path to getting such approval isn't totally out of reach.

On the flip side, even a staking integration in ETH spot ETFs might turn out not to be the needle-moving catalyst we highly anticipate. ETH staking rewards are currently ~3.4% APY, compared to a PoS blockchain like Solana with ~8.17% APY. Issuers have begun filing for spot Solana ETFs too. And if Solana sees an early spot ETF approval (with the current crypto-friendly SEC leadership, I'm not taking anything off the table), it means there will be intense competition among these major altcoins for spot ETF dominance. If by some chance staking gets approved for both Ethereum and Solana spot ETFs, Solana’s higher APY will likely mean diminishing appeal for Ethereum.

I feel Ethereum lacks a fresh, defining catalyst at the moment - the type we saw during the Merge and introduction of liquid staking in late 2022, which saw ETH rise from around $1,500 at the time of the Merge to over $4,000 in 2024. One of the main catalysts we look forward to at the moment - staking integration into the spot ETFs - isn't a sure thing yet. And even if it got greenlit, a ~3% APY might not be compelling enough for some investors, considering the volatility risk inherent in crypto. Investors might feel they are better off with REIT-focused ETFs that offer even higher yields.

Ethereum’s Pectra upgrade is forthcoming (in early May) and would potentially bring lower fees and faster transaction confirmation time to the Ethereum network - a good one for ETH. However, EIP 7251, a core part of the Pectra upgrade, which will increase the validators staking limits from 32 ETH to 2048 ETH, raises some centralization concerns, because we could witness consolidation of the smaller validators with 32 ETH staked, into fewer validators with 2048 staked ETH. Centralization concern is a big deal because it influences governance decisions on the network. And based on this, Ethereum may just be on its way out of being the trustless, decentralized network that offered the foundation for DeFi and decentralized apps, which has been the network's main selling points over the years.

data.title: SmartRent: Further Drop In Price Makes Us Load Up The Truck
data.description:
SmartRent (NYSE:SMRT) is a provider of smart home and smart property solutions for the multifamily industry. Since its IPO via SPAC in 2021, the company has had a challenging time with unclarity of its vision mounting in the CEO stepping down in mid-2024. After just 2 months with the new CEO Shane Paladin at the helm, the company just announced the resignation of this new CEO as well. Since our last article, the stock price massively dropped over 50%, putting the company deep into penny stock territory, primarily as a reaction to the CEO stepping down unexpectedly.

Since Our Last Article

Since our last article just two months ago, a lot has happened. SmartRent reported their Q4 2024 alongside their full year 2024 results.

While Hardware revenue sharply declined, the company's most important revenue stream, their SaaS revenue, continues to show strong growth at 26% y/y for the full year 2024. The growth in SaaS stems from a combination of rising SaaS ARPU (up 4% y/y) and rising number of units deployed (up 12% y/y).

For the full year 2024, the company has used around $33m cash in operating activities. While this cash burn is substantial, their revenue mix was much more healthy in 2024, with almost 42% of the company's revenue coming from Hosted Services in full year 2024.

This shift in revenue mix rhymes well with the company's renewed focus on SaaS revenue, with the incoming CEO Shane Paladin mentioning in the Q4 2024 call, there is a:

...significant opportunity to expand [recurring SaaS revenue]

Also, CFO Daryl Stemm said:

[SmartRent continues its] strategic transformation toward a SaaS focused business model.

Taking a look at their balance sheet, the company still has an impressive and healthy balance sheet, with over $142m in cash and a total of over $257m in current assets, compared to just $130m in total liabilities.

On 31st March, the company also published a press release announcing some AI-powered features in their smart operations system. In the announcement, SmartRent talks about three new features, and this further showcases their push in their SaaS offering.

The most impactful announcement, however, was the resignation of the CEO less than two months after onboarding the company. While the press release does not specify the reasons of the separation, it does say that the resignation happened "at the request of the board". Following this announcement, the share dropped almost 30% in a day to just 80 cents per share.

What To Look Out For

Given their elevated cash burn in 2024 due to costs in connection to a one-off legal matter and separation costs from the prior CEO, we assume that given their strong cash balance, SmartRent will have around 5 years to execute on their turnaround, providing sufficient runway and strong downside protection for investors.

Again, the company is looking for a CEO to steer its turnaround. We are looking out for any potential news as to the reason for the resignation of Paladin, and also for news surrounding a new CEO. Since the company managed to continue to grow their SaaS business even without a CEO at the helm before, we believe that the company can still execute the turnaround with interim-CEO John Dorman at the helm for now.

For the coming quarters, it is especially important to see if SmartRent can be successful in its quest to increase recurring SaaS revenue and improve efficiency. Hardware Revenue should increasingly be seen as a side-business for SmartRent to sell more SaaS contracts. While Hardware revenue still makes up the strongest revenue stream, its Gross Profit margin sat at under 30% in full-year 2024, compared to a SaaS profit margin of over 74%.

If SmartRent is able to significantly reignite SaaS growth with their latest efforts, we see a path to cash flow break-even by 2026.

Risks

The main risk, of course, is the potential inability of the company to continue growing their SaaS revenue or running out of cash before the break-even point. Aside from what, we believe, are obvious leadership issues, these risks could put the company out of business.

Also, tariffs on China could increase prices for smart home devices. We were unable to find any information on where a lot of the hardware SmartRent sells is produced, but we assume there could be some exposure to China. A stark increase in hardware prices could limit SmartRent's ability to grow SaaS revenue beyond already deployed units.

Valuation

Amid greater market uncertainty and a steep decline in share price, we continue to stick to our original valuation model. Citing a median ARR of 7.0x, ignoring SmartRent's hardware and professional services income stream, it would put SmartRent at a $420m valuation at a $60m ARR. This translates to a SMRT share price of just above $2.1, or an upside of around 200% to current prices.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Oakmark Select Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
First-quarter highlights

Top contributors

  • Intercontinental Exchange (ICE)
  • Deere and Company (DE)
  • Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP)

Top detractors

  • Alphabet Cl A (GOOG,GOOGL)
  • Lithia Motors Cl A (LAD)
  • IQVIA Holdings (IQV)

New purchases

  • Molina Healthcare (MOH)

Final sales

  • Fiserv (FI)
  • Salesforce (CRM)

FIRST QUARTER, 2025

  • The Fund’s Investor Share Class (MUTF:OAKLX) returned -1.15% versus -4.27% for the S&P 500 Index for the quarter, and 11.73% since inception versus 9.57% for the index over the same period.
  • At the sector level, the largest contributors to performance were industrials and financials, while consumer discretionary and communication services were the largest detractors.
  • As we have done in other periods when stock prices declined significantly, we are taking advantage of the opportunity to reposition the portfolio toward companies with the most attractive risk-adjusted returns.

TOP CONTRIBUTOR | DETRACTOR

Top contributor

Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) was the top contributor during the quarter. The U.S.-headquartered financial exchange and data company’s stock price rose as it reported solid fourth-quarter 2024 results led by 16% growth in its energy futures business. After a couple years of using cash flow to pay down debt from its two major mortgage acquisitions Ellie Mae and Black Knight, management resumed share repurchase in the first quarter of 2025. We believe share repurchases are an attractive capital allocation opportunity for ICE today. We continue to view ICE as a well-managed, high-quality business with a bright future ahead.

Top detractor

Alphabet was the top detractor during the quarter. The U.S.-headquartered company’s stock price declined despite having posted fourth-quarter 2024 earnings that were in line with consensus expectations. Search revenue growth remained strong, and management reiterated that the new “AI Overviews” feature is driving higher engagement with comparable monetization. The one miss during the quarter was in the Cloud segment, where revenue grew 30% year-over-year but fell slightly short of consensus expectations. We believe the shortfall was largely due to short-term capacity constraints and the long-term growth outlook for Google Cloud remains robust. We continue to see Alphabet as a collection of great businesses that can further benefit from the company’s world class AI capabilities. With shares trading at just 15x our estimate of next year’s earnings per share, we believe the stock is meaningfully undervalued.

PORTFOLIO ACTIVITY

New purchases

Molina Healthcare is a leading managed care company. Molina is the fourth largest player in managed Medicaid but has consistently delivered industry-leading growth and margins. In our view, this is thanks to the company’s exceptional management team and culture of operational excellence. We think Molina has a long runway for growth via continued share gains in Medicaid and untapped opportunities in their Medicare and Marketplace business segments. Recently, fundamentals have been pressured by an unprecedented redeterminations cycle in Medicaid, and valuations have compressed due to uncertainty around policy changes in a new political administration. We believe earnings pressure from redeterminations is temporary and any Medicaid policy changes will prove manageable, providing us an opportunity to purchase shares at a meaningful discount to intrinsic value.

data.title: Sentiment Slumps On U.S. Stagflation Fears
data.description:
By James Knightley

Confidence plunges as households worry about inflation and jobs

The Consumer Confidence Index published by the University of Michigan dropped 6.2 points to 50.8 in early April, below the 53.8 consensus, with expectations falling 5.4 points and current conditions down 7.3. There was a marked deterioration in every subcomponent across all income, age and political affiliation demographics. The survey was conducted 25 March-8 April so the cut-off was just ahead of President Trump’s latest tariff announcement changes that switched more of the focus to China.

The big move was in price expectations, with 1Y ahead inflation predictions jumping to 6.7% from 5% and 5-10Y ahead now at 4.4% versus 4.1% in March. The bond market really doesn’t like this, and Treasury yields are spiking. It also likely limits the Federal Reserve’s ability to cut interest rates anytime soon despite the worrying outlook for consumer spending that today’s data underscores.

Prices, incomes and wealth are moving in the wrong direction

Consumer are facing problems on three key fronts: 1) tariffs will put up prices and squeeze spending power, 2) government spending cuts are raising concerns about jobs and entitlements, and 3) falling stock and bond markets are eroding household wealth. So that means prices, incomes and wealth are all moving against the household sector and it is understandable why they are feeling anxious.

Consumers expect unemployment to rise

Regarding expectations for unemployment, the balance held at +53% saying unemployment will rise over the next 12M. That is 67 percent expecting a rise (on a par with 2009 levels) less 14% expecting a fall. We have only been at these levels on four occasions in the past 50 years. This paints a terribly gloomy picture for the outlook for consumer spending, which accounts for 70% of all the spending in the US economy. Next week’s retail sales are likely to be strong as consumers apparently spent large in March to get ahead of tariffs based on auto, iPhone and consumer electronic sales evidence, but will likely crater in subsequent months as higher prices kick in. Recession risks are real.

Index reading for the question "Is government doing a good job fighting inflation and/or unemployment"?

Interestingly, President Trump appears to be getting much of the blame, with 67% of respondents saying the government is doing a "poor job" on fighting inflation and unemployment. Only 18% say it is doing a "good job". Those are 2008 GFC levels of unhappiness about the government's performance, and worse than when inflation spiked under President Biden's tenure in 2022.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: Oakmark Global Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
First-quarter highlights

Top contributors

  • Lloyds Banking Group (LYG)
  • Prudential (PRU)
  • American International Group (AIG)

Top detractors

  • Alphabet Cl A (GOOG,GOOGL)
  • Diageo (DEO)
  • Kering (OTCPK:PPRUF)

New purchases

  • Airbnb Cl A (ABNB)
  • Brenntag (OTCPK:BNTGF)
  • Pernod Ricard (OTCPK:PDRDF)
  • Sysco Corporation (SYY)

Final sales

  • ETSY
  • Kroger (KR)
  • Novartis (NVO)

FIRST QUARTER, 2025

  • The Fund’s Investor Share Class returned 4.39% versus -1.79% for the MSCI World Index for the quarter, and 9.10% since inception versus 6.21% for the index over the same period.
  • At the sector level, the largest contributors to performance were financials and health care, while communication services and materials were the largest detractors.
  • Geographically, the top three country allocations are 53.7% in the U.S., 9.8% in Germany and 8.4% in the U.K. For the quarter, the U.S., the U.K. and Germany were the top contributors to relative performance. France was a top detractor from relative performance, in addition to Spain and Italy, which were not owned in the portfolio.
  • Emerging markets accounted for 3.2% of the portfolio.

TOP CONTRIBUTOR | DETRACTOR

Top contributor

Lloyds Banking Group was the top contributor during the quarter. The U.K.-headquartered diversified bank’s stock price rose throughout the quarter as it posted fiscal-year 2024 results where net-interest income modestly outperformed consensus expectations. In addition, Lloyds issued fiscal-year 2025 and 2026 guidance forecasting robust net-interest margin expansion and announced a 1.7 billion GBP (Great Britain Pound) share buyback. We continue to monitor the Motor Vehicle provision following the onerous appellate court ruling and are optimistic about a favorable ruling from the Supreme Court. In our view, the bank has a strong management team and a balance sheet with high levels of capital, liquidity and reserves which can help it unlock further value.

Top detractor

Alphabet was the top detractor during the quarter. The U.S.-headquartered interactive media and services company’s stock price declined despite having posted fourth-quarter 2024 earnings that were in line with consensus expectations. Search revenue growth remained strong, and management reiterated that the new “AI Overviews” feature is driving higher engagement with comparable monetization. The one miss during the quarter was in the Cloud segment, where revenue grew 30% year-over-year but fell slightly short of consensus expectations. We believe the shortfall was largely due to short-term capacity constraints and that the long-term growth outlook for Google Cloud remains robust. We continue to see Alphabet as a collection of great businesses that can further benefit from the company’s world-class AI capabilities. With shares trading at just 15x our estimate of next year’s earnings per share, we believe the stock is meaningfully undervalued.

PORTFOLIO ACTIVITY

New purchases

Airbnb is an online marketplace to list, discover and book unique accommodations worldwide. The company benefits from a strong network effect between its guests and hosts. We believe there is a long growth runway as global travel is an attractive market, and alternative accommodations have been taking share. We anticipate Airbnb will drive further growth by creating more valuable services for both sides of its network. This includes the potential for paid placement, which has created significant economic value for comparable marketplaces. In our view, management is aligned with shareholders and well qualified to lead Airbnb as the company attempts to capture these growth opportunities. Short-term concerns about the macro travel environment and declining margins stemming from growth investments allowed us to purchase shares at a discount to our estimate of business value.

Brenntag is a global leader in the production and distribution of specialty and commodity chemicals. In our view, Brenntag is an advantageously positioned specialty chemicals distributor thanks to its robust network of customers and suppliers, which is a market that has historically grown faster than broader chemical manufacturing. We believe this market is coming out of a cyclical trough with indicators suggesting better growth to come. Moreover, the company's management team is implementing a multi-year plan to reorganize the business structure to better align with the distinct needs of specialty and commodity markets, increase focus on value-over-volume, sharpen incentives, and trim the company’s cost structure. Recent temporary challenges in the business environment have weighed on Brenntag's share price, presenting us with the opportunity to initiate a position in the dominant industry player at a discount to our estimate of intrinsic value.

Pernod Ricard is the second largest producer of premium spirits globally, with over 240 brands distributed across more than 160 markets. Importantly, the company generates most of its revenue from brown spirits, such as whiskey and Cognac, and has the highest exposure in the sector to this category, which is particularly attractive due to high barriers to entry driven by aged inventories. Pernod Ricard also has the most regionally diversified portfolio among peers, with leading scale in its key markets of India, China and France. This further strengthens barriers to entry and has provided a long history of attractive returns and healthy cash conversion for the company. Additionally, we believe the company is poised to benefit from structural tailwinds stemming from premiumization trends and emerging markets growth where it has more exposure than its peers. Lastly, we like that Pernod Ricard is still led by the company’s founding family, which is aligned with shareholders as they own 14% of the equity. We believe CEO Alexandre Ricard has a good track record and is committed to growing shareholder value by improving execution, operations and organizational culture, which we view as self-help opportunities to close the company's margin gap relative to peers. The company's share price has come under pressure alongside other spirits producers as a result of post-Covid-19 industry de-stocking and demand normalization, which provided an attractive entry point to invest in this above-average company at a below-average price.

Sysco Corporation is one of the largest food service distribution companies globally. We appreciate that food service distribution is a stable industry that has historically grown slightly faster than food spending. This is because of a continued secular shift to food away from home and that scale-advantaged companies, like Sysco, benefit from better procurement terms from suppliers and demonstrate high operational efficiency. Recently, growth in the company’s largest and most profitable customer segment, independent restaurants, has slowed, but Sysco is increasing headcount and changing compensation incentives in its salesforce, which we think will reinvigorate growth and prove beneficial for long-term shareholders. In our view, Sysco remains a high-quality company with a credible management team, so we were happy to purchase shares at a discount to peers and the company’s own historical trading multiple.

PORTFOLIO MANAGERS

data.title: Alaska Airlines' 2025 Just Got A Lot More Cloudy (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Just three months ago, I wrote in this Seeking Alpha article that I believed Alaska Airlines (NYSE:ALK) had a bright future in 2025. It is no surprise that stock markets have tanked over the past week and airline stocks have fallen at even greater rates than the market in general. The buy rating I made has turned into a 34% decline, almost three times the rate of decline for the S&P 500. I made similar buy ratings on other airlines that have fallen just as badly. Later in the first quarter, I started to issue hold ratings and even those were optimistically generous.

Airlines are always at the pointed end of the spear of economic uncertainty and market declines. Investors understand the current macroeconomic and geopolitical issues but the specifics of not just the airline industry but also for Alaska Airlines are probably less known. This article serves to not only reassess the elements of my previous article but also to help determine where ALK stands with respect to the rest of the airline industry.

Delta Air Lines (DAL) reported its first quarter 2025 financial results on Wednesday, April 9. DAL is almost always the first airline to report, so its financial releases garner lots of attention; its guidance and outlook is usually indicative of what investors will find with other airlines. Delta had already guided to a reduced first quarter profit and largely came close to its revised guidance. Its guidance, however, is what caught everyone's attention. Delta pulled its full-year guidance because it doesn’t have the certainty regarding how 2025 will play out. It says it is already seeing weakness in domestic coach demand, the same segment that has been problematic for the industry for two years – most of the post-Covid airline recovery period.

An Unfinished Covid Restructuring

Part of the reason for the challenges facing the airline industry – and specifically the domestic airline industry - is that significant federal grants were given to the industry during Covid – which started in a presidential election year. Airline employees are a mostly unionized workforce that are heavily concentrated in a number of key states; aid to airlines came in the form of reimbursement for retaining airline employee jobs that would otherwise have been at least temporarily removed. Aid was given under the justification that airlines needed to be able to respond to demand when it returned and, domestically, it began to return in force in early 2022 with international demand coming soon after. The end result was that the economic restructuring that a macroeconomic event such as Covid should have produced did not occur.

Further, airline labor costs soared as airlines had to hire new workers to replace many skilled pilots and mechanics that reached retirement age or accepted retirement packages during Covid. Although demand returned, fares had to increase to cover the higher labor costs, and the industry largely has not yet reached a point of equilibrium between increased labor costs and increased productivity. Domestic economy traffic has suffered because of inflationary pressures that have eroded the spending power of many people in the bottom economic half of the U.S.

As of the end of 2024, ALK was one of just three large U.S. airlines that had a net profit margin greater than 2% with ALK’s 3.37% NI margin bested by United Airlines (UAL)’s 5.5% and DAL’s 5.9%.

ALK is essentially a hybrid carrier – with a lower cost structure than its other legacy carrier peers, but with domestic first class cabins that have been part of how carriers that offer multiple cabins have performed better than domestic competitors that have single class cabins on most of their aircraft. ALK does not presently have longhaul international flights or wide body longhaul aircraft under its own name but gained both of those as part of its merger with Hawaiian Airlines.

Mergers are Messy Affairs

The Alaska/Hawaiian merger was driven by ALK’s need to build scale in an industry where size and scope are key factors of success. ALK operated most of its domestic capacity from its Seattle hub and headquarters, but has smaller hubs up and down the West Coast. It gained a hub at Honolulu and flights across the North and South Pacific via Hawaiian.

Airline mergers are inherently messy and have a fairly high rate of failure when measured by the amount of profit growth and retention of the merged network. Indeed, Alaska acquired Virgin America, a San Francisco-based startup airline that operated for about a decade until it was acquired by ALK in 2016. Alaska decided not to retain Virgin America’s reclining premium cabin seats that competed for upscale premium traffic in the transcontinental U.S. market, resulting in significant share loss in the markets that ALK acquired from Virgin America.

Virgin America was intended to expand Alaska’s network out of the Pacific Northwest and Alaska but faced tough competition from United at San Francisco and American, Delta and United at Los Angeles. Delta tried to convince ALK to become a feeder airline for DAL’s Seattle to Asia network but, with ALK’s decision not to partner further with Delta, DAL set up its own hub at Seattle. ALK carries about twice the number of passengers as DAL carries from SEA; ALK has far more frequencies and destinations, especially in the western U.S. while DAL’s advantage has been its network from SEA to the eastern U.S. and its international network. Delta is the largest international carrier at SEA and adds even more flights through its joint venture partners across the Atlantic and Pacific. The Hawaiian merger is an attempt by ALK to more effectively compete against DAL and UAL, at least in the western U.S.

Airline mergers involve a lengthy process of integrating workforces under federal labor law and of obtaining FAA approval to merge operations, given that airlines have very different and unique operating standards and protocols. Labor integration almost always involves pay increases for the combined workforce. ALK’s workforce has historically been paid less than the big 4 airlines – including Southwest – even though nearly all of ALK’s employees live in much more costly areas of the country. ALK is still early in the process of integrating its workforces. Operational integration is underway, but Hawaiian operates three aircraft types which ALK does not, and so that process will look much more like the multi-year process that other legacy carriers have experienced. The result is that efficiencies cannot be obtained and costs continue to grow while the two “sides of the house” continue to operate.

Alaska has already made significant changes to its network and flight schedules, increasing aircraft utilization and expanding connectivity between the Alaska and Hawaiian systems. There remain a number of opportunities it can explore, including attempting to regain the position that Virgin America once had in a number of east coast markets by using some of the widebody aircraft that have more premium cabins.

High Risk Expansion to Asia

One of ALK’s most significant moves is the announcement of its intention to add flights from Seattle to Tokyo and Seoul by partly moving some of the service that Hawaiian operated from Honolulu to those destinations. However, the Pacific has consistently been the least successful part of U.S. carrier networks. Over the past quarter-century, the U.S. airline industry was profitable on a cumulative basis for 18 of those 25 years, with Latin America (which includes the Caribbean and Central America) profitable for the same number of years. Cumulatively, U.S. airlines were profitable flying the Atlantic just half of those 25 years, while they were profitable across the Pacific for just 9 years. Delta (including its predecessor Northwest) and United traded places as the first and second-largest carriers across the Pacific during the first quarter of this century and were profitable for 13 of those 25 years, but Hawaiian was right at the industry average with only 9 years of transpacific profitability in a quarter-century. The odds are heavily stacked against ALK that it will be able to generate not just consistent profits flying the Pacific, but also profits high enough to invest in the products necessary to win the high-value customers which DAL and UAL have already won over on their global networks including to Asia.

Add in that Asia is heavily targeted in the tariff battle, and transpacific networks for all carriers will come under significant pressure. Cargo revenues are certain to decline. The big 3 countries of NE Asia (Japan, S. Korea and Taiwan) are reportedly aggressively approaching U.S. authorities to negotiate new trade agreements, recognizing the value of military cooperation with the U.S. for each of those countries. Tokyo is the primary connecting hub for American and United and their partners, while Delta and its partner Korean Airlines hub in Seoul. All of those countries serve as major connecting hubs to destinations deeper in Asia – and it is far from certain that other countries in Asia will be as successful in winning negotiations with the U.S. compared to the big 3 NE Asian countries.

Alaska is not presently part of any joint ventures, including in Japan or S. Korea, so ALK will be starting service to two cities against competitors that have established joint ventures in an environment of potentially significant rearrangements of trade. And ALK will be using Hawaiian’s A330 aircraft that does not have anywhere near the amenities that established transpacific carriers have on their fleets. Given the likelihood that trade negotiations will last 90 days or more, there will be enormous uncertainty in transpacific markets as ALK starts transpacific operations from Seattle.

Domestic Expansion is Highly Problematic Now

The final piece of ALK’s strategic plan involves shifting connecting traffic from Seattle to Portland, OR in order to be able to accommodate more connecting traffic for its international and Hawaii flights at SEA. Like UAL, ALK has guided to above industry average capacity growth for 2025 (among carriers that have provided capacity guidance); if the commentary given by DAL about softness in domestic coach traffic, it will be much more difficult for ALK or any airline to successfully add capacity. It is very possible that ALK, like DAL, will move to a fairly tight capacity environment in order to keep yields from following amidst expected economic weakness.

Unlike the larger network carriers, including DAL and UAL, ALK routes nearly all of its connecting traffic through Seattle. ALK will already be at a competitive disadvantage compared to the larger carriers in that its Asia flights will connect just through Seattle, while there are multiple gateways to Tokyo and Seoul on other airlines. Thus, trying to increase capacity in order to facilitate connections leads to a higher risk of depressing local market yields.

Too Many Moving Parts

Although Alaska Airlines possesses a strong management team, they had very lofty plans for 2025. In the midst of economic uncertainty, reducing the number of moving parts in a company’s strategy is an advantage, but that is contrary to what ALK has planned up to this point for 2025. Given the increased economic uncertainty, especially around trade which heavily involves Asia, it is prudent to pull ALK back to a HOLD, even If trade issues are resolved with minimal additional negative impact.
